comments:
Summary:
This paper presents a suite of practical and theoretically grounded improvements to optimal Dec-POMDP planning based on GMAA*. The main contributions are (1) lossless incremental clustering (IC) of action-observation histories within collaborative Bayesian games (CBGs), (2) incremental expansion (IE) of GMAA* search nodes using an incremental CBG solver (yielding GMAA*-ICE when combined with IC), and (3) a hybrid (tree/vector) representation for PWLC heuristics (QPOMDP/QBG) to reduce memory. The authors provide proofs of admissibility/completeness/search-equivalence, algorithmic details (including modifications to BaGaBaB), and an extensive empirical evaluation showing order-of-magnitude improvements in solvable horizons and some increases in agent scalability.

Strengths:
- Strong contributions that address key practical bottlenecks in heuristic optimal Dec-POMDP planning. The PE clustering criterion is clean, lossless, and accompanied by proofs showing it preserves best-response behavior and optimality.
- Incremental expansion is an effective idea for high branching-factor nodes; the paper proves search-equivalence and completeness and integrates it with an incremental CBG solver.
- The hybrid heuristic representation is a practical innovation that meaningfully extends the horizon for which tight heuristics (QBG/QPOMDP) can be stored and used.
- Extensive empirical evaluation across standard benchmarks, demonstrating substantial improvements over prior art and on many problems achieving previously infeasible horizons. The paper also performs several analyses (clustering sizes, node expansions, memory plots) that help interpret results.
- Theoretical treatment is solid: lemmas and theorems are provided and sketched proofs appear sensible and complete in the appendix.

Weaknesses / Opportunities for improvement:
- Heuristic computation remains a bottleneck for several domains. While the hybrid representation alleviates memory, computing the heuristics (vector backups, pruning) can still be time-consuming; the paper notes this but could provide more quantitative breakdowns of heuristic vs search time across problems and horizons (some numbers are given but a more systematic per-domain breakdown would be helpful).
- Some comparisons use different implementations or machines (authors note differences for DP-IPG, MILP setups). While unavoidable to some degree, more controlled or normalized timing comparisons would strengthen claims. In particular, showing wall-clock dominated by the same codebase components where possible (e.g., heuristic computation amortized) would help.
- The PE clustering check is pairwise over types and in worst case may be expensive; although incremental clustering reduces cost and empirical overhead is small, a clearer complexity/empirical profile of clustering overhead as horizon increases would help readers assess limits.
- Scaling in number of agents is improved but still limited; experiments scale up to 6 agents on one family. Discussion of how the methods interact with factored/structured models (e.g., more explicit exploitation of weak coupling, connections to influence-based abstraction) could be expanded with concrete ideas for further agent-scaling.
- The paper is dense and technical; notation is heavy. While proofs are present, some readers may appreciate a more compact worked example tracing IC+IE on a tiny toy instance to build intuition (Dec-Tiger example is used but a complete small-step illustration would help).

Overall assessment and suggestions:
This is a high-quality, well-motivated, and original contribution to Dec-POMDP planning. The techniques are theoretically justified and practically impactful; they substantially advance the state-of-the-art in optimal planning horizons and provide a framework that will be useful both for exact and approximate methods. I recommend acceptance and an oral presentation. For revision or future work the authors should consider: (i) a more detailed breakdown of runtime components (heuristic compute vs search vs clustering) per benchmark and horizon; (ii) providing guidance/heuristics for practitioners on when clustering will be effective a priori; (iii) exploring tighter/incremental heuristic computation methods or online heuristics to reduce offline heuristic cost; (iv) an extended discussion or experiments on integration with factored/weakly-coupled representations for larger agent counts.

Minor: ensure reproducibility details (available code, parameter settings, machine specs) are clearly stated and that the pseudocode and algorithm descriptions correspond exactly to the released implementation.
